questions.question: Which statement best expresses the main idea of the passage?

options.A: Safety data sheets should be locked in a central office.
options.B: Standardized pictograms have replaced written labels entirely.
options.C: Most workplaces have eliminated hazardous chemicals.
options.D: Workers must be informed about chemical hazards so they can protect themselves.

questions.explanation: The passage repeatedly stresses that the point of labels, data sheets, and training is to keep workers informed so they can protect themselves.

questions.question: According to the passage, what does a safety data sheet provide that a label does not?

options.A: Exhaustive detail about properties, health effects, handling, and emergencies.
options.B: A signal word such as 'Warning' or 'Danger.'
options.D: The product's name.

questions.explanation: The passage contrasts the label's 'quick summary' with the data sheet, which is 'exhaustive' and covers properties, health effects, handling, and emergencies.

questions.question: Employers must keep safety data sheets within a worker's reach during her shift most likely because

options.A: safety data sheets are legally required to be displayed in public areas.
options.B: workers are more likely to read the sheets if they are nearby.
options.C: the information is most needed at the moment something goes wrong, when there is no time to search for it.
options.D: the sheets contain information that labels deliberately leave out.

questions.explanation: The passage says data sheets cover emergencies such as a spill or a splash in the eyes, and separately that they must be reachable during a shift rather than locked away. Putting those together, the reason for the location rule is that the information is needed the instant something goes wrong. D describes what the sheets contain, which is true but does not explain where they are kept.

questions.question: The passage is primarily a description of

options.A: why medieval guilds treated boys unfairly.
options.B: how the apprenticeship system trained craftsmen through three stages.
options.C: the French origin of the word 'journeyman.'
options.D: the steps for producing a masterpiece today.

questions.explanation: The passage lays out the apprenticeship as an old system that advanced a learner through three stages: apprentice, journeyman, and master.

questions.question: As used in the passage, the word "indenture" most nearly means a

options.A: daily wage.
options.D: travelling journeyman.

questions.explanation: The passage defines indenture as a contract that 'set out how many years the boy would serve.'

questions.question: The author's main purpose in the final paragraph is to

options.A: argue that guilds should be brought back.
options.B: criticize modern apprenticeships for paying wages.
options.C: explain how to produce a masterpiece.
options.D: contrast old and modern apprenticeships while noting an enduring idea.

questions.explanation: The final paragraph notes what has changed ('modern apprenticeships pay wages... governed by law') yet stresses the 'remarkably durable' underlying idea.

questions.question: Which statement best captures the main idea of the passage?

options.A: A suspension bridge works by passing a load through its parts down into the ground.
options.B: Suspension bridges are more fragile than beam bridges and often collapse.
options.C: The towers are the only part of a suspension bridge that carries weight.
options.D: Anchorages are the parts that carry traffic across the span.

questions.explanation: The passage traces how load moves from deck to suspenders to cables to towers to anchorages, 'all the way into the earth.'

questions.question: What is the role of the anchorages?

options.A: They carry traffic across the span.
options.B: They transfer load from the deck to the suspenders.
options.D: They hold the ends of the cables fast, resisting the cables' inward pull.

questions.explanation: The passage states the anchorages 'hold the ends of the cables fast, resisting the constant inward pull.'

questions.question: As used in the passage, "compression" most nearly means a force that

options.A: pulls a material apart.
options.D: buries a concrete block.

questions.explanation: The passage defines compression as the force that 'pushes them together,' contrasted with tension, which pulls apart.

questions.question: The passage suggests that the towers alone could not hold the bridge up because

options.A: they can carry weight downward but cannot resist the cables' inward pull.
options.B: they are built from materials that are strong in tension but weak in compression.
options.C: they support the deck directly rather than through the suspenders.
options.D: they are too tall to remain stable without additional support.

questions.explanation: The towers carry load straight down through compression, but the main cables pull down and inward, and it is the anchorages that resist that inward pull. Combining those facts, the towers handle only the downward part of the load. B reverses the passage's definitions of tension and compression.

questions.question: In what year did Congress make Labor Day a national holiday?
questions.explanation: The passage states, 'in 1894, Congress made the first Monday in September a national holiday.'

questions.question: The author suggests that the benefits of the modern workweek were

options.A: granted freely by employers.
options.C: won gradually and at real cost by workers.
options.D: invented in the twenty-first century.

questions.explanation: The final paragraph states these comforts 'were not granted freely' but 'were won, gradually and at real cost.'

questions.question: The "four to one" rule concerns a ladder's

options.A: maximum weight rating.
options.C: angle when leaned against a wall.
options.D: material.

questions.explanation: The passage describes the four-to-one rule for a leaned ladder: 'for every four feet of height, the base should sit one foot out from the wall' — a matter of angle/setup.

questions.question: The passage suggests that carrying tools by hand while climbing is discouraged because it

options.A: voids the ladder's duty rating.
options.C: is required by the four-to-one rule.
options.D: makes it harder to maintain three points of contact.

questions.explanation: The passage links keeping hands free (via a tool belt or hoist) to maintaining three points of contact, implying hand-carrying undermines it.

questions.question: The author's main point about ladder safety is that it depends on

options.A: consistently performing simple precautions.
options.B: learning a rare and difficult technique.
options.D: buying expensive equipment.

questions.explanation: The passage concludes that safety is 'not knowing some rare technique but performing ordinary precautions every single time.'

questions.question: The passage is mainly about

options.A: why rain is dangerous to cities.
options.C: the difference between snow and hail.
options.D: how water continuously moves through stages driven by the sun.

questions.explanation: The passage describes the water cycle as an endless movement through stages, 'driven by the sun.'

questions.question: The process by which plants release water vapor from their leaves is called

options.A: condensation
options.D: evaporation

questions.explanation: The passage states plants 'add to this moisture through transpiration, releasing water vapor from their leaves.'

questions.question: According to the passage, condensation is best described as

options.A: a liquid turning to gas.
options.D: a gas returning to liquid.

questions.explanation: The passage calls condensation 'the reverse of evaporation: a gas returning to liquid.'

questions.question: The statement that a summer rain is 'literally ancient' is meant to emphasize that

options.A: rain only forms during summer.
options.B: Earth's water is continually reused rather than newly made.
options.D: the sun is no longer needed for the cycle.

questions.explanation: The passage says water 'is never created or destroyed' and has cycled 'for billions of years,' so the rain is reused ancient water.

questions.question: Who greeted Marisol when she was hovering near the door?

options.A: The woman who ran the tool crib
options.D: A tall man named Dale

questions.explanation: The passage says 'a tall man in a worn canvas jacket' crossed the room and introduced himself as Dale.

questions.question: The ending suggests that Marisol's greatest worry before the job was

options.A: that the work would be too easy.
options.D: that the building would smell of oil.

questions.explanation: The final lines name 'the thing she had dreaded most — not belonging,' which then 'failed to happen.'

questions.question: In the final sentence, the phrase "made room" most nearly means the workers

options.A: gave her more space to park.
options.D: sent her home early.

questions.explanation: In context, 'made room' contrasts with making things 'easy' and resolves her fear of not belonging, so it means they accepted her.

questions.question: The passage is mainly concerned with

options.A: the daily schedule of the president.
options.B: the deliberately lengthy, multi-step process by which a bill becomes law.
options.C: the reasons committees should be abolished.
options.D: the history of one particular famous law.

questions.explanation: The passage walks through each stage a bill must clear and stresses the process was 'designed to be slow.'

questions.question: The passage suggests the lawmaking process is slow because the founders wanted to

options.A: give the president complete control.
options.B: let bills become law without debate.
options.C: prevent the government from acting too rashly.
options.D: keep committees from studying bills.

questions.explanation: The passage states the founders reasoned 'a government which could act too easily might act too rashly.'

questions.question: Before standard time, how was noon in a given town determined?

options.A: By the moment the sun stood highest overhead.
options.D: By the town to the west.

questions.explanation: The passage says 'Noon was simply the moment the sun stood highest overhead.'

questions.question: Why did the railroads, rather than the government, first impose standard time?

options.A: The government had banned time zones.
options.B: The mismatched local clocks created a direct safety hazard for trains.
options.D: Towns preferred the railroads' authority.

questions.explanation: The passage explains mismatched clocks could put 'two trains... on the same track at the same moment,' a hazard the railroads needed to solve.

questions.question: The final sentence implies that the law adopting standard time

options.A: was the true origin of time zones.
options.C: confirmed a practice that commerce had already established.
options.D: was rejected by most cities.

questions.explanation: The passage says the government 'confirmed in law what commerce had already made a fact of daily life.'

questions.question: The central claim of the passage is that

options.A: college degrees are worthless.
options.D: treating a four-year degree as the only worthy path has done real harm.

questions.explanation: The author argues against 'treating it as the only worthy path' while explicitly saying college is not worthless.

questions.question: The author's attitude toward the skilled trades is best described as

options.A: respectful and advocating for them.
options.D: hostile.

questions.explanation: The author defends the trades against being treated as a 'consolation prize' and argues they deserve respect.

questions.question: The author would most likely agree that steering reluctant students toward degrees

options.A: is the best way to end trade shortages.
options.B: can burden them with debt for credentials they cannot use.
options.D: should be done more aggressively.

questions.explanation: The passage warns such students accumulate 'debt for credentials that do not lead to work they enjoy.'

questions.question: As used in the passage, "ennobles" most nearly means

options.A: pays a salary for.
options.D: shortens the path to.

questions.explanation: The author contrasts an education that 'ennobles' with one that 'merely settles,' so ennobles means confers dignity or worth.

questions.question: According to the passage, why is wind described as "solar power in disguise"?

options.A: The sun heats the air unevenly, creating the pressure differences that cause wind.
options.B: Wind turbines contain small solar panels.
options.D: Wind is stored inside the sun.

questions.explanation: The passage explains 'the sun heats the air unevenly, and the resulting differences in pressure set the wind in motion.'

questions.question: The passage is primarily concerned with

options.A: arguing that fossil fuels should be banned immediately.
options.C: comparing solar panels to wind turbines only.
options.D: explaining renewable energy sources and their advantages.

questions.explanation: The passage surveys several renewable sources and notes their shared advantages of endurance and low pollution.

questions.question: The reference to storing and distributing energy suggests that a current limitation of renewables is that

options.A: they will soon run out like fossil fuels.
options.B: their supply can be inconsistent, as when the sun sets or wind drops.
options.C: they produce more pollution than coal.
options.D: they cannot generate electricity at all.

questions.explanation: The passage notes 'the sun sets and the wind is not constant,' implying inconsistency that storage technology aims to address.

questions.question: According to the passage, arteries carry blood

options.A: toward the heart.
options.D: backward through valves.

questions.explanation: The passage states 'Arteries carry blood away from the heart.'

questions.question: The passage suggests that veins contain valves because

options.A: the blood they carry is under lower pressure than the blood in arteries.
options.B: their walls are thinner and more likely to collapse.
options.C: they carry blood that has already released its oxygen.
options.D: they are located farther from the heart than arteries.

questions.explanation: The passage states that veins return blood at lower pressure and that many contain valves preventing backflow, but never links the two. Low pressure is what makes a one-way mechanism necessary. C is true of veins but has nothing to do with the valves.
